podcasts.subtitle: Product | Career | Growth
podcasts.blurb: 面向产品和创业从业者，聚焦增长策略、职业发展与顶尖实践。

episodes.title: Why Netflix is betting on systems thinkers—not specialists—in the AI era | Elizabeth Stone (CPTO)
episodes.description:
Elizabeth Stone is the Chief Product and Technology Officer (CPTO) at Netflix, where she oversees Engineering, Product, and Design. Since her first appearance on the podcast two years ago—which remained my second-most-popular episode for more than a year—she has expanded her role to lead product, in addition to engineering. Before Netflix, Elizabeth was VP of Science at Lyft, Chief Operating Officer at Nuna, an economist at Analysis Group, and a trader at Merrill Lynch.
In our in-depth conversation, we discuss:
1. Why “systems thinking” is now the most important skill she looks for
2. How to manage the flood of AI-generated output without losing quality or signal
3. How Netflix thinks about AI fluency as a universal expectation rather than a level-specific skill
4. What “excellence as an operating system” means
episodes.descriptionCN:
Elizabeth Stone 是 Netflix 的首席产品和技术官 (CPTO)，负责监督工程、产品和设计。自从两年前她第一次出现在播客中（这一集在一年多的时间里一直是我第二受欢迎的节目）以来，除了工程之外，她还把自己的角色扩大到了产品领导。在加入 Netflix 之前，Elizabeth 曾担任 Lyft 的科学副总裁、Nuna 的首席运营官、Analysis Group 的经济学家以及 Merrill Lynch 的交易员。
在我们的深入对话中，我们讨论：
1. 为什么“系统思考”现在是她寻找的最重要的技能
2. 如何在不损失质量或信号的情况下管理大量人工智能生成的输出
3. Netflix 如何将 AI 流畅性视为一种普遍期望，而不是特定级别的技能
4.“作为操作系统的卓越性”是什么意思

episodes.title: How tech workers actually feel about AI in 2026 | Annual AI sentiment survey (Noam Segal)
episodes.description:
Noam Segal is a longtime research leader across Airbnb, Meta, Twitter, Zapier, Intercom, and Figma, a certified coach, AI builder, and my community research lead. Together, we run the annual Tech Worker Sentiment Survey, now in its second year and one of the largest of its kind: a quantitative study of how people in tech actually feel about their jobs, AI, burnout, and the future of their careers. This year’s survey captured responses from thousands of workers across product, engineering, design, research, marketing, data, and sales, and the results are striking.
In our in-depth conversation, we discuss:
1. Why AI has split the tech workforce almost exactly in half—one half that’s thriving, another that’s shaken
2. The four emotional archetypes defining tech workers right now (the Energized, the Conflicted, the Disoriented, and the Resentful)
3. Why burnout has jumped an alarming 11 points in a single year
4. Why nobody in tech would recommend their job to someone entering the industry today
5. The #1 fear in tech right now (it’s not job loss to AI)
6. Why managers are the single biggest lever for employee well-being
7. Concrete advice for what employees and leaders can do right now
episodes.descriptionCN:
Noam Segal 是 Airbnb、Meta、Twitter、Zapier、Intercom 和 Figma 的长期研究领导者、认证教练、人工智能构建者和我的社区研究领导者。我们共同开展了年度科技工作者情绪调查，该调查现已进入第二年，也是此类调查中规模最大的一项：一项定量研究，旨在了解科技人员对自己的工作、人工智能、职业倦怠以及职业未来的实际感受。今年的调查收集了产品、工程、设计、研究、营销、数据和销售领域数千名员工的反馈，结果令人震惊。
在我们的深入对话中，我们讨论：
1. 为什么人工智能几乎将科技劳动力一分为二——一半欣欣向荣，另一半则动摇
2. 目前定义科技工作者的四种情感原型（精力充沛、冲突、迷失方向和怨恨）
3. 为什么职业倦怠在一年内猛增了惊人的 11 个百分点
4. 为什么科技界没有人会向当今进入该行业的人推荐他们的工作
5. 目前科技领域的第一大恐惧（不是人工智能导致失业）
6. 为什么管理者是员工福祉的最大杠杆
7. 关于员工和领导者现在可以做什么的具体建议

episodes.title: Adam Mosseri: AI is a tailwind for authenticity
episodes.description:
Adam Mosseri is the Head of Instagram, where he oversees an app used by over 3 billion people. He also leads the team building Threads. Adam has run Instagram for longer than its founders did, after taking over from Kevin Systrom and Mike Krieger in 2018. A designer by training, he spent over 15 years at Meta, starting as a designer on Facebook’s mobile app, rising to lead Facebook’s News Feed, and eventually chosen to lead Instagram. During his tenure, Instagram’s user base has more than tripled.
In our in-depth conversation, we discuss:
1. How the canonical product team structure is changing in 2026, from baker’s-dozen specialist teams to lean pods of four to six generalists
2. The rise of the “product staff” role—a blending of PM, design, data science, and research into one generalist operator
3. Why Adam is bullish on designers even as functional boundaries dissolve, and which roles are most at risk
4. What the Instagram algorithm knows about you, and why it’s only now catching up to what people assumed it knew years ago
5. Why the rise of AI-generated content is a tailwind for Instagram, and how the company is thinking about creator identity in a synthetic-content world
6. The two biggest product failures of Adam’s career—Facebook Home and the first version of Reels
episodes.descriptionCN:
Adam Mosseri 是 Instagram 的负责人，他负责管理一款超过 30 亿人使用的应用程序。他还领导团队构建 Threads。 Adam 于 2018 年接替凯文·斯特罗姆 (Kevin Systrom) 和迈克·克里格 (Mike Krieger) 后，经营 Instagram 的时间比其创始人还要长。作为一名设计师出身，他在 Meta 工作了超过 15 年，从 Facebook 移动应用程序的设计师开始，逐渐领导 Facebook 的 News Feed，并最终选择领导 Instagram。在他任职期间，Instagram 的用户群增加了两倍多。
在我们的深入对话中，我们讨论：
1. 2026 年规范产品团队结构将如何变化，从十几个面包师专家团队到四到六名通才组成的精益团队
2.“产品人员”角色的兴起——将 PM、设计、数据科学和研究融合为一个通才
3. 为什么 Adam 在职能界限消失的情况下仍看好设计师，以及哪些角色面临的风险最大
4. Instagram 算法对你的了解，以及为什么它现在才赶上人们多年前的认知
5. 为什么人工智能生���内容的兴起对 Instagram 来说是顺风车，以及该公司如何考虑合成内容世界中的创作者身份
6. Adam 职业生涯中最失败的两个产品——Facebook Home 和 Reels 的第一个版本

episodes.title: OpenAI Codex lead on the new shape of product work | Andrew Ambrosino
episodes.description:
Andrew Ambrosino leads development of the Codex desktop app at OpenAI. Nearly 100% of OpenAI employees—not just engineers—now use Codex weekly. A lifelong builder with a background spanning engineering, design, product management, and founding companies, he is now responsible for turning the Codex desktop experience into what he calls “the best desktop app that has ever existed, full stop.”
In our in-depth conversation, we discuss:
1. Why AI has completely flipped the product development process
2. What “taste” really means as a professional skill, and why it is emerging as the most valuable capability in an AI-first workplace
3. Why Andrew believes the Codex app would have failed if they launched it last November (vs. in February)
4. The “zone defense” model for how product managers at OpenAI operate when everyone can build anything
5. How roles are collapsed on Andrew’s team, and why eliminating the concept of roles entirely is a big mistake
6. How Andrew uses Codex to run his own workflows
7. The vision for a home base that coordinates work across ChatGPT, Codex, and the tools people already use.
episodes.descriptionCN:
Andrew Ambrosino 在 OpenAI 领导 Codex 桌面应用程序的开发。近 100% 的 OpenAI 员工（不仅仅是工程师）现在每周都使用 Codex。作为一名终身建设者，他拥有工程、设计、产品管理和创办公司的背景，现在负责将 Codex 桌面体验转变为他所说的“有史以来最好的桌面应用程序，句号”。
在我们的深入对话中，我们讨论：
1. 为什么人工智能彻底颠覆了产品开发流程
2.“品味”作为一项专���技能的真正含义是什么，以及为什么它正在成为人工智能优先的工作场所中最有价值的能力
3. 为什么 Andrew 认为如果去年 11 月（相对于 2 月）推出 Codex 应用程序就会失败
4. 当每个人都可以构建任何东西时，OpenAI 的产品经理如何运作的“区域防御”模型
5. 安德鲁团队中的角色是如何崩溃的，以及为什么完全消除角色的概念是一个很大的错误
6. Andrew 如何使用 Codex 运行自己的工作流程
7. 建立一个协调 ChatGPT、Codex 和人们已经使用的工具的工作大本营的愿景。

episodes.title: What happens after coding is solved? | Fiona Fung (Manager of the Claude Code and Cowork Teams)
episodes.description:
Fiona Fung leads the teams behind Claude Code and Cowork at Anthropic (overseeing Boris Cherny and the entire engineering and PM team). Before Anthropic, she spent 11 years at Microsoft building Visual Studio and TypeScript and then moved to Meta, where she started Facebook Marketplace (now generating over $100 billion in GMV annually), worked on Meta’s first smart glasses and AR glasses, and led infrastructure, growth, integrity, and safety teams at Instagram. She’s been an engineer for over 25 years and has a unique perspective on how the role of building software is changing.
In our in-depth conversation, we discuss:
1. What she’s learned about running a team that’s shipping 8x more code than before
2. Which roles AI will transform next
3. Specific ways her team uses AI
4. How Claude “routines” have changed how she operates as a manager
5. The context-switching problem no one has solved yet
6. The biggest unsolved problem in AI
7. What keeps her up at night
episodes.descriptionCN:
Fiona Fung 领导 Anthropic 的 Claude Code 和 Cowork 背后的团队（监督 Boris Cherny 以及整个工程和 PM 团队）。在加入 Anthropic 之前，她在 Microsoft 工作了 11 年，负责构建 Visual Studio 和 TypeScript，然后转到 Meta，在那里创办了 Facebook Marketplace（目前每年的 GMV 超过 1000 亿美元），开发了 Meta 的首款智能眼镜和 AR 眼镜，并领导了 Instagram 的基础设施、增长、完整性和安全团队。她担任工程师已超过 25 年，对于构建软件的角色如何变化有着独特的视角。
在我们的深入对话中，我们讨论：
1. 她从管理一个交付的代码比以前多 8 倍的团队中学到了什么
2. AI接下来将转变哪些角色
3. 她的团队使用人工智能的具体方式
4. 克劳德的“惯例”如何改变了她作为经理的运作方式
5. 还没有人解决的上下文切换问题
6. 人工智能中最大的未解决问题
7. 是什么让她彻夜难眠

episodes.title: The hidden pattern behind successful products | Mark Pincus (founder of Zynga)
episodes.description:
Mark Pincus founded Zynga—the company behind Words With Friends, FarmVille, and Zynga Poker—and has arguably created more hit consumer products than anyone in history. At Zynga, eight of 10 major game launches became massive hits, reaching over a billion players. Over the past five years, Mark has been synthesizing everything he’s learned about building successful consumer products and turning it into a book, Life at the Speed of Play, which comes out on June 23. This is the first interview he’s done about the book.
In our in-depth conversation, we discuss:
1. His “Proven, Better, New” framework: copy what’s proven, make it better so that 10 out of 10 people say “f*ck yes, I’ll use this”—then add something new
2. Why being less ambitious is the path to the most ambitious ideas
3. His rule of thumb that your instincts are right 95% of the time, but your ideas are wrong 75% of the time
4. “Kill hope before hope kills you”
5. How to raise kids in the age of AI
episodes.descriptionCN:
马克·平卡斯 (Mark Pincus) 创立了 Zynga——Words With Friends、FarmVille 和 Zynga Poker 背后的公司——并且可以说创造了历史上最热门的消费产品。在 Zynga，发布的 10 款主要游戏中有 8 款获得了巨大成功，覆盖了超过 10 亿玩家。在过去的五年里，马克一直在综合他所学到的关于构建成功消费产品的所有知识，并将其写成一本书《Life at the Speed of Play》，该书于 6 月 23 日出版。这是他第一次接受有关这本书的采访。
在我们的深入对话中，我们讨论：
1. 他的“经过验证的、更好的、新的”框架：复制经过验证的内容，使其更好，以便十分之十的人说“f*ck，是的，我会用这个”——然后添加新的东西
2. 为什么不那么雄心勃勃是实现最雄心勃勃的想法的途径
3.他的经验法则是你的直觉95%的时间是正确的，但你的想法75%的时间是错误的
4.“在希望杀死你之前先杀死希望”
5.人工智能时代如何养育孩子

Proposals range from voluntary frontier-model standards with pre-release testing to international coordination, yet critics warn regulatory tools could empower state surveillance.

GPT Live introduces full‑duplex voice with background model orchestration for real‑time translation, tutoring, and conversational assistants. episodes.title: How AI Changes if Open Source Gets Banned
episodes.description:
Potential Chinese limits on overseas distribution of open-weight models could recast frontier AI as a national security asset and reshape global model access. Consequences for token efficiency and cost structures point toward heavier reliance on post-training tuning, lightweight open models, and proprietary alternatives. Coverage includes GPT 5.6 and Fable 5 comparisons, SpaceXAI's Grok 4.5, Meta's Muse Image, Microsoft's Frontier Tuning, and the rise of model routers to balance capability, cost, and governance.

episodes.title: 🔬 RL with Verifiable Rewards, but the Verifier is a Lab — Lila Sciences
episodes.description:
Andy Beam (CTO) and Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli (Co-founder & CSO of Physical Sciences) of Lila Sciences join us to talk about building scientific superintelligence.
Andy makes the case that the internet is a spent resource ("we have but one internet. It's the fossil fuel. We fracked"), and that the next internet-scale dataset comes from running the scientific method as reinforcement learning, with the wet lab as verifier. Science becomes an "infinite token generator" — the lab isn't the product, the model is. The counterintuitive result: one general model trained on ~10 trillion experimentally-verified reasoning tokens across biology, chemistry, and materials beats the domain-specific ones — "breadth gives us depth."
Great blog post from Escalante Bio referenced in the episode: "Your Experiment has a Runtime" (
Highlights:
- The lab as data center: instruments on "a PCI bus," humans "below the API line"
- A CAR-T candidate designed in six months by two or three people
- "Monster UTRs" hitting ~10x Moderna/Pfizer mRNA expression
- The "zero-FTE startup" business model
- "You can't have scientific superintelligence if you're just a good test taker"
- Rafa's "bittersweet lesson": "only the things that you can scale matter"
- Why there's still no AlphaFold for materials
- RL pathologies: collapsed chains of thought, a model that "swears"
- A vision-language model driving a Windows 95 instrument
- "The world's largest collection of voided warranties in biology"
episodes.descriptionCN:
Lila Sciences 的 Andy Beam（首席技术官）和 Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli（物理科学联合创始人兼首席科学官）与我们一起讨论构建科学超级智能。
安迪认为互联网是一种耗尽的资源（“我们只有一个互联网。这是化石燃料。我们进行了水力压裂”），而下一个互联网规模的数据集来自运行强化学习的科学方法，并以湿实验室作为验证者。科学变成了“无限的代币生成器”——实验室不是产品，模型才是。违反直觉的结果是：一个在生物学、化学和材料领域经过大约 10 万亿个经过实验验证的推理标记训练的通用模型击败了特定领���的模型——“广度赋予我们深度”。

episodes.title: The Future of AI Infra: from Kubernetes to Agent Sandboxes — Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
episodes.description:
From adding GPUs a year before ChatGPT to building the cloud primitives behind elastic inference, agent sandboxes, post-training, and production AI workloads, Modal has quietly become one of the most important infrastructure companies in AI. In this episode, Modal CTO Akshat Bubna joins swyx and Vibhu after Modal’s Series C to unpack why AI applications don’t fit traditional cloud assumptions, why Kubernetes was never designed for bursty compute-heavy workloads, and why Modal is now shifting from developer experience to agent experience.
We go deep on Modal’s AI infra stack: serverless functions, decorator-based infrastructure, elastic inference for custom models, GPU snapshotting, DeFlash, speculative decoding, Auto Endpoints, sandboxes, persistent storage, networked containers, private IPv6, RDMA, multi-node training, and Modal’s capacity pool across 17 cloud providers. Akshat also explains why RL rollouts can require 100,000 sandboxes, why production agents need hard guardrails, why observability may matter more than reading code, and why AI has made infrastructure exciting again.
We discuss:
• Why Kubernetes wasn’t built for bursty AI workloads
• How Modal started as a better runtime before becoming an AI cloud
• Why Modal added GPUs a year before ChatGPT
• The shift from developer experience to agent experience
• Why observability matters when agents are writing the code
• Elastic inference for custom models across audio, video, robotics, and comp bio
• GPU snapshotting, cold starts, and why inference workloads are so bursty
• Why RL rollouts can require 100,000 sandboxes
• DeFlash, speculative decoding, and frontier-level inference performance
• Auto Endpoints and making optimized inference easier to deploy
• What Modal adds beyond vLLM, SGLang, and raw GPU rental
• Modal’s 17-cloud capacity pool and “supercloud” strategy
• Networked sandboxes, sidecars, private IPv6, and RDMA
• Serverless multi-node training for post-training and research workloads
• Auto-research, model-guided sweeps, and agents launching GPU experiments
• Compute strategy, capacity planning, and batch tiers
• Why production agents need specialized sandboxes and hard guardrails
• Modal’s take on managed agents, CI, Gitpod/Ona, Python, TypeScript, and Modal Bench

episodes.title: 🔬 "The Most Innovative Diffusion Research Is Happening in Drug Discovery, Not Image Generation"
episodes.description:
Evan Feinberg and Genesis CTO Sergey Edunov join us to talk about solving drug discovery with AI. Sergey, fresh off leading Llama 2 and Llama 3 pretraining at Meta, makes the case that the most interesting architecture work in AI right now isn't happening in language models — it's happening in 3D structure prediction, where diffusion turned out to be the missing primitive the field had been waiting for. Genesis's new model, PEARL (Place Every Atom at the Right Location), puts that to work: it doesn't just predict where a ligand binds, it models how the protein itself flexes to accommodate it. We get into why that was so hard to do until now, and why Evan thinks the field's favorite benchmark — 2Å RMSD — is mostly "slop." (Full technical report here:
We also dig into Genesis's agentic drug discovery system, SAPPHIRE, and what it actually takes for an AI agent to act like a chemist: reasoning about poses, forming hypotheses, reading literature, and proposing the next round of candidates. Plus: why finding a good drug is less "needle in a haystack" and more "hay in a needle stack," the tension between binding affinity and solubility, and how PEARL performed zero-shot on the brand-new OpenBind benchmark ( against a notoriously hard induced-fit target.
episodes.descriptionCN:
Evan Feinberg 和 Genesis 首席技术官 Sergey Edunov 与我们一起探讨利用 AI 解决药物发现问题。 Sergey 刚刚在 Meta 领导 Llama 2 和 Llama 3 预训练，他认为目前人工智能中最有趣的架构工作并不发生在语言模型中，而是发生在 3D 结构预测中，其中扩散是该领域一直在等待的缺失原语。 Genesis 的新模型 PEARL（将每个原子放置在正确的位置）将其付诸实践：它不仅预测配体的结合位置，还模拟蛋白质本身如何弯曲以适应配体。我们将了解为什么迄今为止这很难做到，以及为什么 Evan 认为该领域最喜欢的基准 - 2Å RMSD - 大多是“坡度”。 （完整的技术报告在这里：
我们还深入研究了 Genesis 的代理药物发现系统 SAPPHIRE，以及人工智能代理实际上需要像化学家一样行事：推理姿势、形成假设、阅读文献并提出下一轮候选药物。另外：为什么寻找一种好药物不再是“大海捞针”，而更多是“大海捞针”、结合亲和力和溶解度之间的张力，以及 PEARL 如何在全新的 OpenBind 基准测试中实现零射击（针对臭名昭著的硬诱导拟合目标）。

Riley Brown posted the first TikTok video about ChatGPT the day it launched. It got 20 million views and took him from zero to 200K followers in less than two weeks. Since then, he's built an audience of 1.5 million across platforms, raised $9 million to co-found a vibe-coding startup in San Francisco, and developed a content system so systematic that a single viral video gets reposted across seven accounts every week for the rest of the year.

episodes.title: He got 190K Instagram followers…and regrets it?
episodes.description:
👇 Build a membership with me in 4 weeks (starts July 3)
Yoni Smolyar posted a video on Instagram every single day for 508 days. Zero followers to start. He didn't tell a soul. What started as a cringe-avoidance experiment turned into a 500+ video archive, a 190K-follower audience, and a software business that made $10K in its first weekend and hit #1 on Product Hunt. This episode is part origin story, part post-mortem, and part philosophy of short-form content in a world with shrinking attention sapns.

episodes.title: The Accidental Genius of the Disney Vault
episodes.description: In 1944, Disney accidentally created one of the greatest business strategies in entertainment history: they re-released Snow White in theaters seven years after its debut. Because home video didn't exist, an entirely new generation of kids experienced the film for the first time, birthing the "Disney Vault." Disney still relies on this 6-8-year cadence today (more in the form of sequels than re-releases today) to keep their core IP culturally dominant without over-saturating the market.
episodes.descriptionCN: 1944 年，迪士尼意外地创造了娱乐史上最伟大的商业策略之一：《白雪公主》在首映七年后重新在影院上映。由于家庭录像不存在，全新一代的孩子第一次体验了这部电影，诞生了“迪士尼金库”。如今，迪士尼仍然依靠这种 6-8 年的节奏（更多地以续集而不是重新发布的形式）来保持其核心 IP 在文化上的主导地位，同时避免市场过度饱和。

episodes.title: AI Software Factories
episodes.description:
“Clearly there are 100x or 1,000x engineers now. The world hasn’t adjusted.”
— Guillermo Rauch (Vercel)
Guillermo: I’ve been really pilled with this idea of software factories. You used to ship the output directly, and everything inside the company was how good is person A at shipping output B? And now what’s happening is the way that I’m judging you as an engineer is, are you producing the factory that will produce multiplicative outputs B through Z, right?
We used to believe, and it used to be somewhat controversial, that there’s 10X engineers. Now clearly there’s 100X or 1,000X engineers. And the world hasn’t fully adjusted to this.
@naval: I used to get flamed on Twitter for saying there are 10X engineers, because it flies in the face of so much equality philosophy that everyone’s equal. But the reality is, when you’re operating in idea domains—when you’re operating in intellectual domains and virtual digital domains—it’s not even 10X. It’s 100X or 1,000X, and it always has been.
Satoshi, Notch, the guy who invented JavaScript, the Brendan Eichs of the world. John Carmack. These are 1,000X programmers.
Not to even mention if you choose the right thing to work on versus the wrong thing to work on, that’s an infinity difference. And it could just be not necessarily even a better programmer, just one who had a better judgment of what to work on in the first place.
And now obviously it’s less controversial, because of AI leverage.
episodes.descriptionCN:
“显然现在有 100 倍或 1,000 倍的工程师。世界还没有调整。”
— 吉列尔莫·劳赫 (Vercel)
Guillermo：我对软件工厂的想法非常着迷。你以前直接运送输出，公司内部的一切都是A在运送输出B时有多好？现在发生的事情是我对你作为一名工程师的评价，你是否正在生产将产生乘法输出 B 到 Z 的工厂，对吗？
我们曾经相信，有 10X 工程师的存在，但也曾经存在一些争议。现在显然有 100 倍或 1,000 倍的工程师。世界还没有完全适应这一点。
@naval：我曾经因为说有 10X 工程师而在 Twitter 上受到攻击，因为它违背了人人平等的平等理念。但现实是，当你在创意领域运作时——当你在知识领域和虚拟数字领域运作时——它甚至不是 10 倍。它是 100 倍或 1,000 倍，而且一直都是。
Satoshi，Notch，JavaScript 的发明者，世界上的 Brendan Eichs。约翰·卡马克.这些是 1000 倍的程序员。
更不用说你选择正确的工作还是错误的工作，这是一个无穷大的差异。而且不一定是更好的程序员，只是一个对首先要做什么有更好判断的人。
现在，由于人工智能的影响力，它的争议显然减少了。

episodes.title: FDE: The $1M/Year AI Job Explained
episodes.description:
I sit down with Vas from Varick Agents to map out exactly how to break into AI forward deployed engineering — and how to grow into a sharper FDE — in thirty days. We start from a single premise: every company can now buy the same frontier intelligence, so the real advantage moves to deployment. Vas traces the role back to Palantir, explains the judgment that decides where AI belongs, and lays out the audit → evals → deployment loop that turns raw models into measurable business value. He then hands over a full 30-day plan to build, harden, measure, and defend a production-grade agent, so you can do the job before you hold the title. The whole conversation stays tactical and grounded, with clear examples I can apply today.
The FDE Blueprint:
Timestamps
00:00 – Intro
02:03 – What is an FDE
04:09 – How Palantir Popularized FDEs
06:16 – Deciding Where Intelligence Belongs
11:26 – What FDEs Earn
14:59 – Two Kinds of Judgment: Communication and Engineering
17:38 – How the Work Really Gets Done
20:40 – Audit, Evaluation, Deployment
22:56 – Which LLM to Choose
27:36 – Audit: Finding the Workflow Worth Rebuilding
31:47 – Evals: Turn non-determinism into evidence
32:57 – Deployment: Build on Existing Systems
38:59 – The 30-Day Plan Begins
49:13 – Final Thoughts
Key Points
* Intelligence is now commoditized, so the real edge lives in deployment — the job of the AI forward deployed engineer.
* Vas traces the FDE role to Palantir, where engineers embed on-site, learn workflows, and customize the ontology per client.
* The strongest FDEs blend deep technical skill with consulting-grade communication — the rare "art plus science" combination worth up to a million dollars a year.
* The FDE loop runs audit → evals → deployment, and each improved workflow makes the next one clearer.
* Vas condenses a year of learning into a 30-day plan: build an agent, harden it, make it measurable, then defend it like an FDE
Numbered Section Summaries
1. Intelligence on Tap Vas opens by showing me that every company can now buy the same frontier intelligence, from Claude Code to Codex to Cursor, which turns the raw model into a commodity. Because everyone taps the same stack, the advantage shifts to where, how, and why a business applies it — the exact territory of the forward deployed engineer.
2. The Palantir Blueprint Vas draws on friends who worked as Palantir FDEs to explain the origin of the role. Palantir built a customizable ontology, sent engineers on-site to learn each client's workflows, and spun up dashboards and agents tuned to that specific business — essentially consulting for the software age.
3. Where Intelligence Belongs We dig into FDE judgment: choosing which steps of a workflow deserve an LLM and which stay as deterministic software or simple if-then logic. Vas cites the MIT figure that 95% of generative AI pilots fail, and credits selective design plus deep on-site observation as the fix.
4. The Million-Dollar Combination Vas frames the FDE as the best of two worlds — a fluent communicator who reads business reality and a strong engineer who ships production systems. I compare it to someone who speaks both art and science, and Vas confirms that this rarity is exactly why roles reach up to a million dollars a year.
5. Audit, Evals, De**ployment** Vas cements the core loop: audit the real workflow, build evals that turn fuzzy tasks into evidence, then deploy on top of existing systems like NetSuite, Salesforce, and SAP. He stresses full audit trails and human-in-the-loop approval so clients trust what the agent does.
6. Selling and De-Risking the Work We talk pricing and trust. Vas suggests running the first audits free to prove measurable value, and I share how my agency LCA rebranded the word "audit" as a "sprint" so it lands more smoothly with clients.
7. The 30-Day Plan Vas splits a year of learning into four weeks: week one builds an agent that completes a real loop; week two hardens it with schemas, failure modes, and exception handling; week three makes it measurable across revenue, risk, and cost; week four defends it like both an engineer and a VP.

我与 Varick Agents 的 Vas 坐下来，详细规划了如何在 30 天内进入人工智能前沿部署工程，以及如何成长为更敏锐的 FDE。我们从一个前提开始：每个公司现在都可以购买相同的前沿情报，因此真正的优势转向部署。 Vas 将该角色追溯到 Palantir，解释了决定 AI 归属的判断，并制定了审核 → 评估 → 部署循环，将原始模型转化为可衡量的业务价值。然后，他提交了一份完整的 30 天计划，用于构建、强化、衡量和捍卫生产级代理，以便您可以在获得头衔之前完成这项工作。整个对话保持策略性和基础性，并提供了我今天可以应用的清晰示例。
FDE 蓝图：
时间戳
00:00 – 介绍
02:03 – 什么是 FDE
04:09 – Palantir 如何普及 FDE
06:16 – 决定智能的归属
11:26 – FDE 的收入
14:59 – 两种判断：通���和工程

20:40 – 审计、评估、部署

27:36 – 审计：寻找值得重建的工作流程
31:47 – Evals：将非决定论转化为证据
32:57 – 部署：基于现有系统构建
38:59 – 30 天计划开始
49:13 – 最后的想法
要点
* 智能现已商品化，因此真正的优势在于部署——人工智能前沿部署工程师的工作。
* Vas 将 FDE 角色追溯到 Palantir，工程师在现场嵌入、学习工作流程并为每个客户定制本体。
* 最强大的 FDE 将深厚的技术技能与咨询级沟通融为一体——罕见的“艺术加科学”组合，每年价值高达 100 万美元。
* FDE循环运行审核→评估→部署，每一个改进的工作流程都使下一个工作流程更加清晰。
* Vas 将一年的学习浓缩为一个 30 天的计划：构建一个代理，强化它，使其可测量，然后像 FDE 一样捍卫它
编号部分摘要
1. Intelligence on Tap Vas 首先向我展示了每个公司现在都可以购买相同的前沿情报，从 Claude Code 到 Codex 再到 Cursor，这将原始模型变成了商品。因为每个人都使用相同的堆栈，所以优势转移到企业应用它的位置、方式和原因——这正是前沿部署工程师的领域。
2. Palantir Blueprint Vas 借鉴了曾担任 Palantir FDE 的朋友来解释该角色的起源。 Palantir 构建了一个可定制的本体，派工程师到现场了解每个客户的工作流程，并启动针对特定业务的仪表板和代理——本质上是软件时代的咨询。
3. 智能的归属 我们深入研究 FDE 判断：选择工作流程的哪些步骤值得 LLM，哪些保留为确定性软件或简单的 if-then 逻辑。 Vas 引用了麻省理工学院的数据，即 95% 的生成式 AI 试点都失败了，并将选择性设计加上深入的现场观察作为解决方案。
4. 价值百万美元的组合 Vas 将 FDE 描述为两个世界中最好的一个：一个能够阅读业务现实的流利沟通者，另一个是负责交付生产系统的强大工程师。我将其与既讲艺术又讲科学的人进行比较，瓦斯证实，这种稀有性正是角色每年达到一百万美元的原因。
5. 审核、评估、部署** Vas 巩固了核心循环：审核真实的工作流程，构建将模糊任务转化为证据的评估，然后部署在 NetSuite、Salesforce 和 SAP 等现有系统之上。他强调完整的审计跟踪和人机交互批准，以便客户相信代理所做的事情。
6. 销售工作并降低工作风险 我们谈论定价和信任。 Vas 建议免费进行第一次审核以证明可衡量的价值，我还分享了我的机构 LCA 如何将“审核”一词重新命名为“冲刺”，以便更顺利地与客户接触。
7. 30 天计划 Vas 将一年的学习分为四个星期：第一周构建一个完成真实循环的代理；第二周通过模式、故障模式和异常处理来强化它；第三周使其可以通过收入、风险和成本来衡量；第四周像工程师和副总裁一样捍卫它。

episodes.description:
In this episode I sit down with Corey Ganim to unpack what he calls the simplest way to earn money with AI in 2026: a $999 AI Tools Assessment for small business owners. Corey lays out his entire four-phase system, from a probing discovery call through an AI-assisted analysis in Claude, a stupid-simple client report, and a review call that turns roughly half of clients into implementation buyers. We walk through his full upsell menu, seven client-acquisition methods that run on zero capital and zero audience, and his AI Concierge retainer that earns him about $1,000 an hour. Anyone listening leaves with a copy-and-paste playbook they can adapt to their own city or industry.
Checkout Corey’s AI Audit Template:
Timestamps
00:00 – Intro and the episode promise
03:48 – The full playbook preview
06:39 – Phase 1: the discovery call
08:27 – Phase 2: AI Analysis
12:11 – Phase 3: The Report
22:24 – Tool and Model Selection
23:39 – Phase 4: The Review Call
26:15 – The Upsell Services
35:52 – Finding Customers
49:16 – The AI Concierge retainer
58:46 – Final Thoughts
Key Points
* The core offer is a $999, 45-minute AI Tools Assessment that prescribes 3–7 off-the-shelf tools, backed by a full-refund guarantee tied to finding at least five reclaimed hours per week.
* Fulfillment runs in four phases: discovery call, AI analysis in Claude, a templatized report, and a review call that converts about half of clients into implementation work.
* The report stays deliberately simple, with an executive summary, an effort-versus-impact matrix, quick wins, a four-day quick-start plan, and a clear ROI slide.
* The upsell menu spans process redesign, automation builds, knowledge systems, custom workflows, and full implementation, with lifetime value reaching $3K–$10K or more.
* Seven client-acquisition methods run on zero capital and zero audience, from local meetups and door knocking to agency partnerships and office hours.
* The AI Concierge retainer ($1,200–$2,000/month for two calls) creates recurring revenue at roughly a $1,000 hourly rate.
Numbered Section Summaries
1. The Assessment Offer Corey sells a 45-minute structured interview to small business owners in the $500K–$5M revenue range, then prescribes 3–7 off-the-shelf AI or SaaS tools to reclaim five to ten hours a week. The full-refund guarantee makes the offer feel risk-free, and at $999 it acts as a foot-in-the-door tripwire.
2. The Four-Phase Fulfillment System Phase one records a discovery call with an AI note-taker like Fathom; phase two feeds that transcript to Claude to surface pain points and research fixes; phase three generates the client report; phase four reviews it live. Corey stresses probing on the first call and saving every prescription for later.
3. Building a Stupid-Simple Report Built in Claude Design from a reusable template, the report leads with an executive summary and an effort-versus-impact matrix that spotlights high-impact, low-effort "quick wins." A four-day quick-start plan and a financial-impact slide make the ROI obvious, and Corey iterated on this piece twelve times to keep it clear.
4. The Upsell Menu Roughly half of assessment clients ask for implementation, which opens five to six upsells: process redesign, automation builds, knowledge systems, custom workflows, and full implementation packages.
5. Turning One-Time Sales Into Retainers Corey shows how each upsell can become a monthly retainer, such as building a set number of Claude skills or automations per month. He also shares a favorite close: crediting the paid assessment toward the upsell while marking the upsell up, so the client feels a win and the math stays profitable.
6. Seven Ways to Find Clients The list covers local AI meetups, door knocking, LinkedIn DMs, free audits for your network, agency partnerships, AI office hours at co-working spaces, and posting your wins.
7. The AI Concierge Retainer His favorite upsell is a done-with-you retainer: two 45-minute calls a month spent building Claude skills and teaching Claude Cowork, plus unlimited Voxer access with a 12-business-hour response window. He closed five of his first six prospects, reached $8K MRR in ten days, and prices new clients from $1,200 to $2,000 a month while capping the roster at six.

episodes.title: Making $$$ with Loop Engineering
episodes.description:
I sit down with Elie Steinbock to unpack loop engineering and how to run a business on loops. We start with the roots of the idea in the lean startup and Toyota's manufacturing, then move into practical, copy-ready workflows for SEO, Facebook ads, and product feedback. Elie walks through a live Google Search Console example on Draft Fantasy and shows how to set up an SEO loop that runs once a month for years. The core promise for listeners: hand repeatable business work to an AI agent that measures an objective metric and improves over time. By the end, you know how loops work and how to launch your first one today.
Timestamps
00:00 – Intro and episode promise
02:54 – What is Loop Engineering
06:51 – Loops with AI agents: build and verify
11:17 – Example of Loop: SEO as an objective-metric loop
15:29 – Setting up the SEO loop and tools
25:27 – Cost and token economics
29:05 – The Paid ads loop
33:10 – The product feedback loop
36:25 – A minimal viable loop for every channel
39:21 – Closing Thoughts
Key Points
* Loop engineering means giving an agent a task, an objective metric, and a stop condition so it improves on a schedule.
* The lean startup and Toyota's build-measure-learn cycle map directly onto AI agents.
* An SEO loop connects to Google Search Console and Data for SEO, then pushes rankings up month over month.
* These loops run cheaply — often a few dollars per monthly run — which beats the cost of an agency.
* The same pattern extends to Facebook ads, and a product feedback loop stands as the ultimate version.
* Start small with a minimal viable loop tied to a clear metric like impressions or ten likes.
Numbered Section Summaries
* The Promise of Running a Business on Loops I open by asking Elie what listeners will walk away with, and he frames the whole episode: use loops to automate SEO, ads, and more. We agree the aim is clear, copyable workflows people can launch today.
* Where Loop Engineering Comes From Elie traces the recent buzz to Boris from Claude Code and Peter Steinberger, plus a joking tweet from his friend Dimitro about software that builds itself. He grounds it in the lean startup's build-measure-learn cycle, which itself grew from Toyota's lean manufacturing.
* Loops With AI Agents: Build and Verify Elie explains the agent version: a build step paired with a verify step and a clear stop condition. He uses Inbox Zero's evals as an example, where the agent keeps adjusting the prompt or model until accuracy passes 90%.
* The SEO Loop We dig into SEO as the flagship example, where Google ranking serves as a clean, objective metric. Elie describes a loop that runs once a month, learns from the last run via a markdown memory file, and steadily climbs the rankings.
* Setting It Up on Real Data Elie shows his Draft Fantasy Search Console, connects the agent to Google Search Console and Data for SEO, and runs the loop live in Codex. He shares the Atom Eve prompt as a deeper template people can copy.
* Cost and Token Economics I raise Ross Mike's skepticism about loop buzz and token spend, and Elie makes the case that an SEO loop stays cheap — often under five dollars per monthly run. He adds that Max-plan users have plenty of headroom, while tight budgets suit cheaper open models like GLM 5.2.
* Ads, Product Feedback, and the Ultimate Loop We move to a Facebook ads loop that tests copy and creative variants, favoring a mix of human hooks and AI optimization. Then Elie describes the product feedback loop — reading customer feedback, analytics, and logs to prioritize and ship — as the closest thing to a business that builds itself.
* Starting Small We close on the minimal viable loop: begin with one channel and a modest, verifiable metric like impressions or ten likes, then let it compound. Elie and I agree that every part of a business could sit on a loop, and starting one today makes for a low-risk experiment.

我与 Elie Steinbock 坐下来探讨循环工程以及如何在循环上经营业务。我们从精益创业和丰田制造的理念根源开始，然后进入用于 SEO、Facebook 广告和产品反馈的实用、可复制的工作流程。 Elie 演示了 Draft Fantasy 上的实时 Google Search Console 示例，并展示了如何设置一个多年来每月运行一次的 SEO 循环。对听众的核心承诺：将可重复的业务工作交给人工智能代理，该代理可以测量客观指标并随着时间的推移进行改进。最后，您将了解循环的���作原理以及如何启动您今天的第一个循环。
时间戳
00:00 – 简介和剧集承诺
02:54 – 什么是循环工程
06:51 – 使用 AI 代理循环：构建和验证
11:17 – 循环示例：SEO 作为目标度量循环
15:29 – 设置 SEO 循环和工具
25:27 – 成本和代币经济学
29:05 – 付费广告循环
33:10 – 产品反馈循环
36:25 – 每个通道的最小可行循环
39:21 – 结束语
要点
* 循环工程意味着为代理提供任务、客观指标和停止条件，以便其按计划进行改进。
* 精益创业和丰田的构建-测量-学习周期直接映射到人工智能代理上。
* SEO 循环连接到 Google Search Console 和 SEO 数据，然后逐月推动排名上升。
* 这些循环运行成本低廉——通常每月运行几美元——这超过了代理机构的成本。
* 同样的模式也适用于 Facebook 广告，产品反馈循环是最终版本。
* 从小处开始，建立一个最小的可行循环，与印象数或十个赞等明确的指标相关联。
编号部分摘要
* 在循环上经营业务的承诺 我首先询问 Elie，听众会得到什么，他框架了整个情节：使用循环来自动化 SEO、广告等。我们一致认为，目标是明确的，人们今天就可以启动可复制的工作流程。
*《循环工程来自 Elie》追溯了 Claude Code 和 Peter Steinberger 最近对 Boris 的热议，以及他的朋友 Dimitro 发布的关于自动构建软件的开玩笑推文。他将其根植于精益初创公司的构建-测量-学习周期，该周期本身是从丰田的精益制造发展而来的。
* 使用 AI 代理循环：构建和验证 Elie 解释了代理版本：构建步骤与验证步骤和明确的停止条件配对。他以 Inbox Zero 的评估为例，其中代理不断调整提示或模型，直到准确率超过 90%。
* SEO 循环 我们将 SEO 作为旗舰示例，其中 Google 排名作为一个干净、客观的指标。 Elie 描述了一个每月运行一次的循环，通过 Markdown 内存文件从上次运行中学习，并稳步攀升排名。
* 在真实数据上进行设置 Elie 展示了他的 Draft Fantasy Search Console，将代理连接到 Google Search Console 和 SEO 数据，并在 Codex 中实时运行循环。他将 Atom Eve 提示分享为人们可以复制的更深层次模板。
* 成本和代币经济学 我提出了 Ross Mike 对循环嗡嗡声和代币支出的怀疑，而 Elie 则认为 SEO 循环保持便宜 — 通常每月运行不到 5 美元。他补充说，Max-plan 用户有足够的空间，而紧张的预算适合 GLM 5.2 等更便宜的开放型号。
* 广告、产品反馈和终极循环我们转向 Facebook 广告循环，测试文案和创意变体，支持人类挂钩和人工智能优化的组合。然后 Elie 描述了产品反馈循环——���读客户反馈、分析和日志来确定优先级并交付——作为最接近于自我构建的业务的东西。
*从小处开始我们关闭了最小的可行循环：从一个渠道和一个适度的、可验证的指标（如印象数或十个点赞）开始，然后让它复合。埃利和我都同意，企业的每个部分都可以处于循环状态，今天开始循环就可以进行低风险实验。

episodes.description:
In this episode I bring Nick Vasilescu, co-founder of Orgo, back on the show to unpack the buzz around Grok 4.5. Nick makes the case for treating Grok 4.5 as a genuine AI co-founder inside harnesses like Hermes and OpenClaw, and he proves it live: spinning up cloud computers, wiring in tools, and building a full startup from idea to landing page to outreach. We race Grok 4.5 against GPT 5.6 Sol, tour Nick's agent stack, and talk through the cost paradox of a model this fast and cheap. Listeners walk away with a concrete playbook for standing up their own always-on agent today.
Get Nick’s Agent Template Stack:
Timestamps
00:00 – Intro
01:40 – Why Grok 4.5 release matters
03:39 – Automation versus a co-founder
05:16 – Setting up Hermes and Grok 4.5 on Orgo
09:01 – Why Orgo to manage Agents
11:23 – Grok 4.5 Cost discussion
14:02 – Grok 4.5 Fast Execution and Unlock
16:20 – The Agent tool belt
19:13 – X MCP for trends
20:37 – vidIQ for outliers and thumbnails
22:11 – Finding new startup ideas
26:15 – Grok 4.5 versus GPT 5.6 Sol
30:56 – Ranking and Reviewing the startup ideas
34:06 – The AI agency opportunity
38:46 – Thumbnails over Telegram
40:10 – Reviewing AI Agency Landing Page
41:58 – Vertical MCPs and agent startups
43:36 – Skill graph and the offer
45:25 – Reviewing the Thumbnail Generated
46:42 – Email Outreach Campaign
48:07 – Reviewing Market Insight 1-Pager
50:45 – From a Camry to a Ferrari
52:12 – Reviewing Cold Email Outreach Sequence
53:22 – Closing thoughts
Key Points
* Grok 4.5 delivers Opus 4.8-level intelligence at a fraction of the cost and roughly 10-15x the speed of Fable.
* I learn to treat the model as a co-founder by handing it email, a phone number, a debit card, memory, and every connector that matters.
* Nick runs agents on Orgo cloud computers so they stay online, textable, and ready around the clock.
* Live, Grok 4.5 builds a landing page in about 40 seconds and wins on design and copy for me over GPT 5.6 Sol.
* The stack ships from idea to website, offer, thumbnail, and cold-email sequence in a single session.
* Nick's take: costs keep dropping while speed and intelligence keep climbing, so building your agent now compounds overnight.
Numbered Section Summaries
1. The Grok 4.5 Moment Nick frames Grok 4.5 as the release that brings frontier-level intelligence to everyday builders. He ties it back to the Opus 4.5 wave that sparked the ClaudeBot and OpenClaw era, and positions Grok as the speed-and-price unlock that makes the whole experience feel brand new.
2. Automation Versus a Co-Founder Nick reframes the goal. Instead of hands-off automation, he treats his Hermes agent, Dewey, as a co-founder with its own computer, email, phone, debit card, and Obsidian vault. The lesson I take: hand the agent more tools and the magic compounds.
3. Standing Up the Stack on Orgo Nick walks through Orgo cloud computers, one-click templates, and SSH access, then spins up a fresh Hermes box powered by Grok 4.5 on command. He shares his own Nix stack template so listeners can launch the same setup in one click.
4. The Agent Tool Belt We tour the connectors that make an agent useful: Agent Mail, Agent Phone, Agent Card, Composio, Latitude for observability, Linear, the X MCP for trends, Idea Browser for taste, and vidIQ for outlier videos and thumbnails. Context, Nick says, is king.
5. Grok 4.5 Versus GPT 5.6 Sol We race both models on a one-page landing site. Grok 4.5 finishes in roughly 40 seconds, and I favor its design and copy, while Sol delivers a solid result moments later. The takeaway: both models shine, and Grok holds the edge on speed and token efficiency.
7. Idea to Business in One Session Dewey generates ten startup ideas, consolidates on a managed AI employee agency, and then builds the landing page, thumbnails over Telegram, market research, and a cold-email sequence in a Google Doc. Idea Browser's skill graph guides the offer toward a weekly client-reporting deliverable.
8. Ride the Wave Nick closes on momentum: prices keep falling as speed and intelligence keep rising, so setting up your agent today earns instant upgrades with every new model. My send-off: get your hands dirty and build.

episodes.descriptionCN:
在本期节目中，我将 Orgo 联合创始人 Nick Vasilescu 重新带回节目，为大家解开有关 Grok 4.5 的话题。 Nick 提出了将 Grok 4.5 视为 Hermes 和 OpenClaw 等工具中真正的 AI 联合创始人的理由，他现场证明了这一点：启动云计算机，连接工具，并建立一个从创意到登陆页面再到推广的完整初创公司。我们将 Grok 4.5 与 GPT 5.6 Sol 进行比较，参观 Nick 的代理堆栈，并讨论如此快速且便宜的模型的成本悖论。听众们在离开时会得到一本具体的剧本，以帮助他们今天站起来永远在线的代理人。
获取尼克的代理模板堆栈：
时间戳
00:00 – 介绍
01:40 – 为什么 Grok 4.5 发布很重要
03:39 – 自动化与联合创始人
05:16 – 在 Orgo 上设置 Hermes 和 Grok 4.5
09:01 – 为什么 Orgo 来管理代理
11:23 – Grok 4.5 成本讨论
14:02 – Grok 4.5 快速执行和解锁
16:20 – 代理工具带
19:13 – X MCP 趋势
20:37 – vidIQ 异常值和缩略图
22:11 – 寻找新的创业想法
26:15 – Grok 4.5 与 GPT 5.6 Sol
30:56 – 对创业想法进行排名和审查
34:06 – 人工智能代理机会
38:46 – Telegram 上的缩略图
40:10 – 审查��工智能机构登陆页面
41:58 – 垂直 MCP 和代理初创公司
43:36 – 技能图和报价
45:25 – 检查生成的缩略图
46:42 – 电子邮件推广活动
48:07 – 回顾市场洞察一页
50:45 – 从凯美瑞到法拉利
52:12 – 审查冷电子邮件外展序列
53:22 – 结束语
要点
* Grok 4.5 以极低的成本提供 Opus 4.8 级别的智能，速度大约是 Fable 的 10-15 倍。
* 我学会将模型视为联合创始人，将电子邮件、电话号码、借记卡、内存和每个重要的连接器交给模型。
* Nick 在 Orgo 云计算机上运行代理，以便他们保持在线、可发短信并全天候做好准备。
* Live 上，Grok 4.5 在大约 40 秒内构建了一个登陆页面，并且在设计和复制方面胜过 GPT 5.6 Sol。
* 该堆栈在单个会话中从创意到网站、报价、缩略图和冷电子邮件序列。
* Nick 的看法：成本不断下降，而速度和智能不断上升，因此现在构建您的代理只需一夜之间即可完成。
编号部分摘要
1. Grok 4.5 时刻 Nick 将 Grok 4.5 视为为日常构建者带来前沿智能的版本。他将其与引发 ClaudeBot 和 OpenClaw 时代的 Opus 4.5 浪潮联系起来，并将 Grok 定位为速度和价格的解锁者，让整个体验感觉焕然一新。
2. 自动化与联合创始人尼克重新制定了目标。他没有将自己的爱马仕经纪人杜威视为联合创始人，而是拥有自己的电脑、电子邮件、电话、借记卡和黑曜石金库，而不是不干涉的自动化。我学到的教训是：给特工更多的工具和神奇的化合物。
3. 在 Orgo 上站立起来 Nick 介绍了 Orgo 云计算机、一键式模板和 SSH 访问，然后根据命令启动由 Grok 4.5 提供支持的新 Hermes 盒子。他分享了自己的 Nix 堆栈模板，以便听众可以一键启动相同的设置。
4. 座席工具带 我们将介绍使座席变得有用的连接器：座席邮件、座席电话、座席卡、Composio、用于可观察性的 Latitude、Linear、用于趋势的 X MCP、用于口味的 Idea Browser 以及用于异常视频和缩略图的 vidIQ。尼克说，背景才是王道。
5. Grok 4.5 与 GPT 5.6 Sol 我们在一页登陆网站上对这两种模型进行了比赛。 Grok 4.5 在大约 40 秒内完成，我喜欢它的设计和副本，而 Sol 稍后提供了可靠的结果。结论：两种模型都很出色，Grok 在速度和代币效率方面保持着优势。
7. 一堂课将创意转化为商业杜威提出了十个创业创意，整合了一个托管人工智能员工机构，然后构建了登陆页面、Telegram 缩略图、市场研究以及 Google 文档中的冷电子邮件序列。 Idea Browser 的技能图表指导每周交付客户报告。
8. 乘风破浪 尼克势头强劲：随着速度和智能的不断提高，价格不断下降，因此今天设置您的代理即可获得每个新型号的即时升级。我的告别：亲力亲为，开始建造。
